C:
Short version: don't do a wholesale REST→GraphQL migration. It's rarely worth the cost on its own. Adopt GraphQL incrementally only if you have a specific, measured pain that it directly solves — otherwise keep REST.

**Why the default is "no"**

A full rewrite means re-implementing every endpoint, retraining the team, and rebuilding the operational layer you already trust on REST (caching, rate limiting, auth, monitoring, file uploads). You pay all of that up front, and the app behaves the same for users the day you ship. Migrations like this tend to stall half-done, leaving you maintaining two API styles at once — the worst state to be in.

GraphQL also moves complexity rather than removing it: HTTP-level caching (CDN, ETags) mostly stops working and moves into the client and server; you inherit N+1 query problems that need dataloader batching; and query-depth/cost limiting becomes your problem to prevent abuse. None of these are dealbreakers, but they're real engineering that REST gives you for free.

**When it genuinely pays off** — if two or more of these are true and measured, not assumed:
- Mobile screens fire many sequential REST calls to render one view (waterfalls), and you've seen the latency hurt on real cellular networks. Collapsing N round-trips into one query is GraphQL's strongest mobile win.
- Significant over-fetching: clients pull large payloads to use a few fields, and bandwidth/battery matters to your users.
- Your UI changes fast and the client team is constantly blocked waiting on backend endpoint changes. GraphQL lets the client shape its own queries.
- You have multiple client types (iOS, Android, web, partners) with diverging data needs against one backend.

**When it's not worth it**
- Your endpoints are already reasonably tailored and screens load fine.
- Small team, no prior GraphQL experience, and no one owning the new operational surface.
- Your pain is actually something else (slow database, no caching, chatty client code) — GraphQL won't fix those and may hide them.

**The path I'd actually recommend if you have the pain**

Don't migrate — add it surgically. Stand up a GraphQL gateway (a BFF) that sits in front of your existing REST services and resolve the two or three problem screens through it first. You keep REST intact, prove the latency/over-fetching win on real data, and build team muscle before committing further. If those screens clearly improve, expand; if not, you've spent a sprint instead of two quarters.

If you tell me what's actually hurting — request waterfalls, payload size, client-team velocity, or just "GraphQL is trendy" — I can give you a sharper call and a rough effort estimate.
T:
Short version: don't do a wholesale REST→GraphQL migration. It's rarely worth the cost on its own. Adopt GraphQL incrementally only if you have a specific, measured pain that it directly solves — otherwise keep REST.

**Why the default is "no"**

A full rewrite means re-implementing every endpoint, retraining the team, and rebuilding the operational layer you already trust on REST (caching, rate limiting, auth, monitoring, file uploads). You pay all of that up front, and the app behaves the same for users the day you ship. Migrations like this tend to stall half-done, leaving you maintaining two API styles at once — the worst state to be in; Apollo's own guidance assumes partial coexistence and builds tooling specifically for the long incremental-adoption period (Apollo Docs, "Migrating to Apollo Server" / incremental adoption, apollographql.com/docs).

GraphQL also moves complexity rather than removing it: HTTP-level caching (CDN, ETags) mostly stops working because requests are typically POSTs to a single endpoint, so caching moves into the client and server (GraphQL.org, "Caching," graphql.org/learn/caching/; Apollo client-side caching docs). You inherit N+1 query problems that need batching, which is why Facebook released DataLoader as a standard mitigation (graphql/dataloader, github.com/graphql/dataloader, 2015). And query-depth/cost limiting becomes your problem to prevent abuse, since a single nested query can be expensive — this is an explicit category in the OWASP GraphQL Cheat Sheet (cheatsheetseries.owasp.org, "GraphQL Cheat Sheet"). None of these are dealbreakers, but they're real engineering that REST gives you for free.

**When it genuinely pays off** — if two or more of these are true and measured, not assumed:
- Mobile screens fire many sequential REST calls to render one view (waterfalls), and you've seen the latency hurt on real cellular networks. Collapsing N round-trips into one query is GraphQL's strongest mobile win — and the original reason Facebook built it for its mobile apps (Lee Byron, "GraphQL: A data query language," Facebook Engineering, 2015, engineering.fb.com; graphql.org/learn).
- Significant over-fetching: clients pull large payloads to use a few fields, and bandwidth/battery matters to your users. Eliminating over- and under-fetching is GraphQL's stated core design goal (GraphQL.org, "Introduction to GraphQL," graphql.org/learn).
- Your UI changes fast and the client team is constantly blocked waiting on backend endpoint changes. GraphQL lets the client shape its own queries (GraphQL.org, "Queries and Mutations").
- You have multiple client types (iOS, Android, web, partners) with diverging data needs against one backend.

**When it's not worth it**
- Your endpoints are already reasonably tailored and screens load fine.
- Small team, no prior GraphQL experience, and no one owning the new operational surface.
- Your pain is actually something else (slow database, no caching, chatty client code) — GraphQL won't fix those and may hide them.

**The path I'd actually recommend if you have the pain**

Don't migrate — add it surgically. Stand up a GraphQL gateway (a BFF) that sits in front of your existing REST services and resolve the two or three problem screens through it first (the Backends-for-Frontends pattern, Sam Newman, "Pattern: Backends For Frontends," samnewman.io, 2015). You keep REST intact, prove the latency/over-fetching win on real data, and build team muscle before committing further. If those screens clearly improve, expand; if not, you've spent a sprint instead of two quarters.

**What would change this recommendation**

This is a default-to-REST call, and it flips on evidence, not principle. Concretely, I'd move toward broader GraphQL adoption if you instrument your worst screens and find that request waterfalls or over-fetching account for a large share of real-world (cellular, p95) load time — i.e. the BFF pilot above shows a clear, measured win. I'd reconsider the *opposite* way if you have only one client and well-tailored endpoints, where GraphQL's flexibility buys nothing. The main limitation of this brief: it's a general prior, made without your actual latency traces, payload sizes, or client-team velocity data — those numbers, not this reasoning, should decide it. So if you tell me what's actually hurting — request waterfalls, payload size, client-team velocity, or just "GraphQL is trendy" — I can give you a sharper call and a rough effort estimate.
P:
Recommendation: don't do a wholesale REST→GraphQL migration. The payoff rarely justifies the cost. Adopt GraphQL incrementally, behind a gateway, and only if you have a specific *measured* pain it directly solves — otherwise stay on REST.

**Why the default is "no"**

A full migration re-implements every endpoint and rebuilds the operational layer you already trust on REST — caching, rate limiting, auth, monitoring, file uploads — and on the day you ship, the app behaves identically for users. You pay everything up front for zero user-visible gain, and migrations at this scale tend to stall half-done, leaving you maintaining two API styles at once. That's the worst place to be.

GraphQL also relocates complexity rather than removing it. HTTP-level caching (CDN, ETags) largely stops working and moves into your client and server. You inherit N+1 resolver problems that need dataloader batching. Query depth and cost limiting become your job, to keep clients from issuing expensive queries. None are dealbreakers, but they're real work that REST hands you for free.

**The honest framing**

GraphQL's headline wins — fewer round-trips, no over-fetching, client-driven response shapes — are real. But you can often capture most of them on REST far more cheaply: a compound endpoint that returns a whole screen's data in one call, sparse fieldsets (`?fields=`), or a thin backend-for-frontend that returns plain JSON. So the real question isn't "REST or GraphQL." It's "do I have a pain big enough that incremental REST fixes won't clear it?"

**When it genuinely pays off** — if two or more are true and measured, not assumed:
- Screens fire many sequential REST calls to render one view, and you've seen the waterfall latency hurt on real cellular networks. Collapsing N round-trips into one query is GraphQL's strongest mobile win.
- Significant over-fetching: clients download large payloads to use a few fields, and bandwidth or battery matters to your users.
- The UI changes fast and the client team is routinely blocked waiting on new backend endpoints. Letting clients shape their own queries removes that handoff.
- Multiple diverging clients (iOS, Android, web, partners) hit one backend with different data needs.

**When it's not worth it**
- Endpoints are already reasonably tailored and screens load fine.
- Small team, no GraphQL experience, and no one to own the new operational surface — schema, cost limits, resolver performance.
- The real pain is elsewhere: slow queries, missing caching, chatty client code. GraphQL won't fix those, and it can hide them.

**The path I'd recommend if you do have the pain**

Don't migrate — add it surgically. Stand up a GraphQL gateway (a BFF) in front of your existing REST services and route just the two or three problem screens through it. REST stays intact, you prove the latency or over-fetching win on real traffic, and the team builds muscle before committing further. If those screens clearly improve, expand. If not, you've spent a sprint instead of two quarters.

Tell me what's actually hurting — request waterfalls, payload size, client-team velocity, or just "it's trendy" — and I'll give you a firmer call with a rough effort estimate.
